detail:
The pattern is real and accurately described. EntityRegistryItemView.patch (apps/tenants/views.py:1090-1145) and .delete (1158-1172) read tenant.pii_entity_map, mutate the dict in Python, and write the ENTIRE field back via Tenant.objects.filter(pk=...).update(pii_entity_map=entity_map) with no select_for_update, no transaction.atomic, and no JSONB-merge expression. The inbound redactor does the symmetric thing: apps/pii/redactor.py:292 reads existing_map = tenant.pii_entity_map (a snapshot from tenant-load time, before the slow NER step), then at lines 412-414 — only when it mints NEW entities — writes merged = {**existing_map, **new_map_entries} over the whole field. I verified there are no locks/atomic/F() expressions in either path. So the race is genuine: if a message is being redacted (its existing_map snapshot still contains placeholder X) and that message mints a new entity, the write-back resurrects X, silently undoing a concurrent user DELETE of X or reverting a concurrent PATCH that corrected X's name. The PIIDenylist add/remove/bulk views (1244-1259, 1278-1287, 1329-1353) have the identical unguarded full-dict overwrite of pii_denylist, so an 'ignore this person' action can be lost the same way. When it fires, the symptom is exactly the privacy failure the People page exists to fix (a corrected/deleted real name reappears and keeps leaking into replies). I downgraded severity from medium to low because the trigger is narrow: the user must be editing the privacy page within the read-modify-write window of a single inbound message AND that message must mint at least one new entity (the redactor does NOT write back when none are found); it is single-tenant, non-corrupting across rows, and user-recoverable (re-delete). The wide NER latency between read and write does widen the window somewhat, so it is not purely theoretical — just low-frequency.</final_detail>
<suggested_fix>Make each writer a row-locked or atomic read-modify-write so the curate-vs-redact writers compose instead of clobbering. Two practical options: (1) wrap each mutation in transaction.atomic() with Tenant.objects.select_for_update().get(pk=...) to re-read the latest map under a row lock, apply the delta, then save only the field — applied to both the views and the redactor write at redactor.py:412-414; or (2) move the merge into the database with a JSONB expression so Postgres does the merge server-side (e.g. for adds, pii_entity_map = pii_entity_map || new_entries via a Func/RawSQL, and for the view DELETE, pii_entity_map - placeholder). Option 2 also fixes the redactor cheaply since it is purely additive. The views' DELETE/PATCH still need the lock-and-re-read (or the '-' / jsonb_set operators) because they remove/replace keys. The denylist views need the same treatment.</suggested_fix>
<fix_complexity>small</fix_complexity>
<evidence">apps/tenants/views.py:1145 — PATCH writes whole map: Tenant.objects.filter(pk=tenant.pk).update(pii_entity_map=entity_map), no lock/atomic</evidence>
</invoke>
evidence: apps/tenants/views.py:1172 — same full-dict overwrite on DELETE
apps/pii/redactor.py:412-414 — `merged = {**existing_map, **new_map_entries}` then `.update(pii_entity_map=merged)` from a stale read
apps/tenants/views.py:1249, 1286, 1353 — pii_denylist writes use the same unguarded full-dict `.update()` pattern
